title.title: Triplet Extraction from Art-historical Texts for Knowledge Graph Creation

author.affiliation: Leibniz-Informationszentrum Technik und Naturwissenschaften, Hannover, Germany
Leibniz-Informationszentrum Technik und Naturwissenschaften, Hannover, Germany
Leibniz-Informationszentrum Technik und Naturwissenschaften, Hannover, Germany
Leibniz-Informationszentrum Technik und Naturwissenschaften, Hannover, Germany

sourceDesc.p: Converted from an OASIS Open Document

keywords.term: digital research infrastructures development and analysis
I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

body.p: Recent developments in image search engines, such as iART (Springstein et al., 2021; Schneider et al., 2022), have assisted art historians in analyzing large image collections using different kinds of similarity. A shortcoming of these systems is the lack of clarity in their methodologies and data sources used to identify visual similarities. They reflect the biases inherent in the data, which focus on a Western canon that mirrors societal imbalances. Our approach seeks to address these limitations by incorporating data from a broader scope of art-historical literature, increasing interpretability, diversity and accuracy of these systems. Vision-language Models (VLMs), such as CLIP ( Contrastive Language-image Pre-training; Radford et al., 2021) are integrated with knowledge graphs to provide users with contextual information, and to improve search accuracy and diversity by incorporating domain-specific knowledge. The knowledge graphs are enriched with structured knowledge from academic and institutional texts, collected to represent a range of art-historical perspectives. We implement various generative models such as REBEL ( Relation Extraction By End-to-end Language Generation; Huguet Cabot and Navigli, 2021) , KnowGL (Knowledge Generation and Linking from Text; Rossiello et al., 2023), GoLLIE (Guideline following Large Language Model for Information Extraction, Sainz et al. 2024) and DeepKE (Deep Knowledge Extraction; Zhang et al. 2022) to extract triplets from these texts and integrate them into a knowledge graph. For the Large Language Models (LLM)-based methods (i.e., GoLLIE and DeepKE), we optimize the prompts for our case in order to increase the performance of extraction and to extract only relations that are relevant to us. In addition, we extract scene graphs from the images contained in the data to extract triplets from the visual information and store them in our knowledge graph.

Pan et al. (2023) provide a review of the integration of LLMs with knowledge graphs and propose a roadmap for unification. Castellano et al. (2021) suggest the use of knowledge graphs to improve results of automated artwork analysis. Our methodology for constructing art-historical knowledge graphs is inspired by Jain et al. (2022), who create domain-specific graphs for triplet extraction and automated linking of entities to Wikidata counterparts.

div.head: Objectives
div.p: The project's objective is to enhance the explainability of image search engine results for art historians by combining VLMs with the explicit semantic information of knowledge graphs. This integration is designed to (i) increase the accuracy and relevance of automated image retrieval through domain-specific knowledge; (ii) provide users with an interface that displays a local scene graph and a global knowledge graph, thereby enhancing transparency; and (iii) promote diversity in art-historical research by using training data that includes underrepresented artists and perspectives. The local scene graph is designed to represent elements within each image, while the global knowledge graph encapsulates broader relationships and contextual insights across the entire data set. Central to our research efforts is the automated generation of these knowledge graphs: leveraging the text processing capabilities of generative models and neural networks for scene graph prediction, we are transforming art-historical information into triplets, which are then stored in a graph database.

p.hi: Our data set contains 120,000 resources from museums such as
as well as encyclopedic materials like
100,000 scholarly articles are selected from academic databases such as

div.head: Pipeline and Current State

p.hi: construction pipeline. We use two methods for triplet construction: (i) Text is extracted, parsed, and translated; for triplet extraction, we use a
are extracted; triplets are generated using a

div.p: At the initial stage, our primary focus is on the extraction of triplets from textual documents. For documents in non-machine readable formats, we use LayoutParser (Shen et al., 2021) to isolate image and text. Given the multilingual nature of our data set, we classify the language of each sentence and then translate it into English using the NLLB-200 model (Costa-Jussà et al., 2022). Triplet prediction from these texts is performed using the afore-mentioned models REBEL, KnowGL, GoLLIE, and DeepKE. Subsequently, entity reconciliation with Wikidata is performed using ReFinED (Ayoola et al., 2022 ) to ensure that results are consistent with the logic of the Wikidata ontology.

Currently we evaluate the efficiency of these generative models in extracting triples. Due to the complexity of the scientific literature, annotation guidelines are being developed to compare human annotations with model outputs. For this, we use the semantic annotation platform INCEpTION (Klie et. al., 2018). These metrics are used to refine prompts for extraction of art-historically significant information. Quantitative evaluations are iteratively applied to assess the validity of triple extraction and establish a benchmark for subsequent research.

We extend the scope of our extraction to include visual content in scientific articles and websites. To achieve this, we plan to use state-of-the-art scene graph prediction methods, in particular VETO ( Vision rElation TransfOrmer; Sudhakaran et al., 2023), to transform visual data into triplets. These triplets will be integrated into our knowledge graph, creating a database that encapsulates both textual and visual information. The pipeline is illustrated in Figure 1.

div.head: Outlook
div.p: Next steps are: (i) by implementing a human-in-the-loop approach, we intend to improve the quality of the extracted triplets; this includes engaging students and experts to review and benchmark the triplets generated by the generative models to ensure their correctness; (ii) by using LLMs, we aim to counter-check the validity of the extracted triplets (Boylan et al. 2024); (iii) We generate an annotated dataset based on the art historical datasets and manually create triplets to evaluate the pipeline; (iv) we plan to construct knowledge graphs on different textual data sets to compare texts from different time periods, e.g., art criticism from the 19th century with essays from the 1980s to 2000s; (v) to enhance diversity actively curating datasets that include contributions from understudied perspectives, promoting equal representation in both textual and visual analyses.

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: This project is funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (German Research Foundation) – project number 510048106.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Ayoola, Tom, Shubhi Tyagi, Joseph Fisher, Christos Christodoulopoulos, and Andrea Pierleoni. 2022. “ReFinED: An efficient zero-shot-capable approach to end-to-end entity linking.” In North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics-Human Language Technology, NAACL-HLT. 2022.

Boylan, Jack, Demian Gholipour Ghalandari, Chris Hokamp, Dominic Thorn, Parsa Ghaffari and Shashank Mangla. 2024. “KGValidator: A Framework for Automatic Validation of Knowledge Graph Construction”, in: Joint proceedings of the 3rd International workshop on knowledge graph generation from text (TEXT2KG) and Data Quality meets Machine Learning and Knowledge Graphs (DQMLKG) co-located with the Extended Semantic Web Conference (ESWC) 2024. 26-30.

bibl.hi: Castellano, Giovanna, Giovanni Sansaro, and Gennaro Vessio.
2021. “Integrating Contextual Knowledge to Visual Features for Fine Art Classification.” In
Proceedings of the Workshop on Deep Learning for Knowledge Graphs

2022. “No Language Left Behind: Scaling Human-Centered Machine Translation.” arXiv.

. 2021. “REBEL. Relation Extraction By End-to-End Language Generation.” In
Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2021
, 2370–81. Punta Cana, Dominican Republic: Association for Computational Linguistics.

. 2022. “Generating Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs. Challenges with Open Information Extraction.” In

Proceedings of the 1st International Workshop on Knowledge Graph Generation From Text and the 1st International Workshop on Modular Knowledge co-located with 19th Extended Semantic Conference, ESWC 2022.
